ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general fried egg class; no specific Vietnamese fried egg with onions entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GG covers fried hen's eggs; onion component not separately coded. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese fried egg with onions; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 150 g, equivalent to 2–3 fried eggs with sliced onions, as commonly plated as a side dish accompanying steamed rice in Vietnamese home cooking.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Egg, whole, cooked, fried (FDC ID 173424) and Onions, raw (FDC ID 170000), combined with HPB Singapore nutrient reference for fried egg dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board, Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese fried egg with onions exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Values are adapted from USDA FDC fried whole egg (FDC ID 173424) as the primary anchor, with onion contribution (USDA FDC ID 170000) and fish sauce sodium adjustment incorporated. The input record's per-100g values (energy 186.67 kcal, protein 12.0 g, fat 14.67 g, carbohydrate 2.67 g) are broadly consistent with this USDA-derived composition for a fried egg dish with onions and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 300 mg/100g reflects fish sauce seasoning and is plausible for this preparation style.

gi_source.title: N/A — food contains negligible carbohydrates; University of Sydney GI Database consulted, no direct entry for this dish
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fried egg with onions contains only 2.67 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Eggs themselves contain negligible carbohydrates. Glycemic index is not applicable for this food as the carbohydrate content is too low to produce a meaningful glycemic response. GL per serving is effectively 0.0 (2.67 g carbs per 100 g × 150 g serving = 4.0 g available carbs; GL = GI × carbs/100 ≈ negligible). University of Sydney GI Database consulted; no direct GI entry for this dish. glycemic_index set to null per the critical q4 rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods — setting to 0 would be incorrect.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g from fish sauce seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request this dish prepared with reduced or no fish sauce and no added salt, as the 300 mg sodium per 100 g (450 mg per standard 150 g serving) approaches 20% of the 2,000 mg daily sodium limit; pairing with unseasoned steamed rice and potassium-rich vegetables can partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.67 g per 100 g) and high protein (12 g per 100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fried egg with onions is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (2.67 g per 100 g) and high protein (12 g per 100 g), which support glycaemic stability; however, the high cholesterol content (373 mg per 100 g) warrants limiting egg consumption to 3–4 eggs per week in those with concurrent cardiovascular risk, and preparation with minimal oil is advised to reduce total fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high cholesterol (373 mg per 100 g) and elevated saturated fat (4 g per 100 g) from eggs and pan-frying oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish with caution due to the very high cholesterol content (373 mg per 100 g; approximately 560 mg per 150 g serving, approaching the 300 mg daily limit in a single serving), elevated saturated fat (4 g per 100 g), and borderline sodium (300 mg per 100 g from fish sauce); frequency should be limited to once or twice per week, preparation with minimal unsaturated oil (e.g., canola) is preferred, and fish sauce should be reduced or omitted.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Egg with Onions (Vietnamese: Trứng Chiên Củ Hành) is a simple, everyday Vietnamese dish consisting of whole eggs pan-fried together with sliced onions in a small amount of cooking oil, typically seasoned with fish sauce and ground black pepper. The dish is a staple side dish (món ăn kèm) in Vietnamese home cooking, commonly served alongside steamed jasmine rice as part of a multi-dish family meal. The eggs develop a lightly crispy, golden-brown edge from pan-frying while the yolk remains semi-set, and the onions soften and caramelise slightly, adding mild sweetness and aromatic depth. Fish sauce contributes umami and saltiness characteristic of Vietnamese cuisine. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (12 g per 100 g) and fat (14.67 g per 100 g), very low in carbohydrates (2.67 g per 100 g), and contains significant cholesterol (373 mg per 100 g) due to the egg yolks. Sodium is elevated at 300 mg per 100 g from fish sauce. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, the dish is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low glycaemic impact, but the high cholesterol and saturated fat content make it a moderate-to-high concern for cardiovascular disease. Hypertensive individuals should request reduced fish sauce. A standard serving is approximately 150 g (2–3 eggs with onions), providing roughly 280 kcal.

The input record's values are broadly consistent with this composition and are retained. Medium confidence reflects the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this specific Vietnamese fried egg with onions preparation are not available as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-16; values would require laboratory analysis of the specific dish. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (2.67 g per 100 g); GI measurement is not meaningful for this dish; glycemic_index is set to null (not 0) per the critical q4 rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. The input record listed two duplicate portion entries (both 150 g, both is_default: true); only one default portion is retained. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed as HPB Singapore but does not resolve to the official HPB nutrient database; USDA FoodData Central was used as the primary approved source instead. Country of origin in the input was listed as Singapore, which reflects the serving context (GrabFood Singapore listing); region of origin is corrected to Vietnam as the dish's culinary origin. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply. q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0 and not a string) as food contains negligible carbohydrates (2.67 g/100g) — this is the correct numeric-null per the critical q4 rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to 'not_applicable'. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0 (numeric). gi_source documents University of Sydney GI Database consultation and explains why GI is not applicable.
